gaps.title: First-tool latency / discoverability impact of ENABLE_TOOL_SEARCH=true

attempts.result: Phase 1 docs already limited the claim to smaller upfront payload; they explicitly said first-tool latency and discoverability were unmeasured locally.
The post adds discoverability evidence because the author says most users would not know the setting exists, but it still gives no first-tool latency benchmark.

evidence.quote: Phase 1 narrowed the claim to upfront payload reduction, not latency or ergonomics.
Q2 remained open because no first-tool latency or discoverability benchmark was present.
The author was unaware of the setting, then described lazy loading and a smaller starting context.

gaps.answer: Discoverability impact is source-supported: the setting was non-obvious and the author only found it after inspecting tool-schema overhead. First-tool latency and task ergonomics remain unmeasured, so the gap narrows but does not fully close.
gaps.residual_unknowns: No first-tool latency, fallback-rate, or task-completion evidence exists for this repo or for the source post.

gaps.title: Edit-retry root-cause attribution across prompt | workflow | guardrail buckets

attempts.result: Phase 1 docs already said the packet only reports the count of 31 edit-retry chains and never partitions causes.
The source post repeats the 31-chain count but gives no examples, labels, or per-chain breakdowns that would support prompt/workflow/guardrail attribution.

evidence.quote: Open question Q8 stayed unresolved because the packet never separated root causes.
Implementation summary says Q8 remained open for lack of partitioned evidence.
The post mentions 31 failed-edit-then-retry sequences and nothing more.

gaps.answer: UNKNOWN. After the doc reread and the source reread, the only recoverable fact is that 31 edit-retry chains existed. There is no evidence to bucket them into prompt, workflow, or guardrail causes without fabricating.
gaps.residual_unknowns: Per-chain examples, error text, or any author-supplied classification are absent from the packet and source.

gaps.title: Net-cost audit of /clear + plugin-memory remedy bundle vs stale-resume baseline

attempts.result: Phase 1 already isolated the problem: the remedy bundle was recommended without subtracting its own hook, context, and skill overhead.
The post makes the overhead surfaces explicit: startup context injection, recall search, and auditor runs, but it still never prices their token or context cost against stale-resume rebuilds.

evidence.quote: F22 says the remedy bundle is one-sided until its own overhead is netted out.
F9 keeps clear-and-rehydrate as prototype-later because the remedy overhead is not netted.
The post says /clear relies on auto-injected context, recall, and an auditor skill.

gaps.answer: The bundle's components are now clearer, but the net-cost question still is not answered. The source names the compensating mechanisms behind /clear, yet never counts the extra injected tokens, hook writes, or recall costs against the stale-resume baseline.
gaps.residual_unknowns: No token-count or prompt-size delta is provided for injected context, recall lookups, or auditor invocation overhead.

gaps.title: Reddit-post arithmetic reconciliation

attempts.result: Phase 1 preserved the mismatches but did not test whether any sub-calculations were locally self-consistent.
The source shows that 264M is a rounded 14,000*22*858 estimate and 54% matches 6,152/11,357, but it never explains 926 vs 858 or 11,357 vs 18,903.

evidence.quote: ADR-002 records the unresolved 926 vs 858 and 18,903 vs 11,357 mismatches.
The 264M figure comes from 14k saved per turn, 22 turns per session, across 858 sessions.
The post separately states 858 sessions, 18,903 turns, 6,152 of 11,357 idle-gap turns, and 12.3M wasted tokens.

gaps.answer: The sub-math is mixed. 264M tokens is locally consistent as a rounded 14,000*22*858 estimate, and the 54% idle-gap ratio matches 6,152/11,357. But the post still never reconciles the 926-session header or why 11,357, not 18,903, is the idle-gap denominator.
gaps.residual_unknowns: Header-vs-body session counts and the 11,357-turn denominator remain permanently source-bounded without external author clarification.

gaps.title: Cross-phase validation of phase 005 auditor/parser vs F14-F17 expectations

attempts.result: Phase 1 expectations were explicit: an offline-first auditor, guarded transcript parsing, policy-aware skill review, and cross-agent sharing at schema/dashboard layers instead of parser monoculture.

Phase 5's shipped design matches those expectations at the contract level: JSONL-to-relational analytics, verifier/discoverer split, policy rejection of raw count<=2 disable thresholds, and dashboard-contract reuse instead of HTML or parser lock-in.

evidence.quote: Phase 1 expected an offline-first auditor and a guarded adapter rather than stable core JSONL infra.
Phase 1 expected reasoned skill review and schema/dashboard sharing instead of a single parser.
Phase 5 documents JSONL ingestion, relational analytics, verifier/discoverer split, and contract-first borrowing.
Phase 5 rejects the raw count<=2 disable widget and says to borrow skill_usage, not the threshold policy.
Phase 5 keeps parsing in an additive reader model with transcript identity, offsets, and replay verification gates.

gaps.answer: Closed at the design-contract level. Phase 5's shipped research matches F14-F17: it defines an offline JSONL-to-analytics auditor, keeps parsing behind a guarded replay model, rejects raw low-usage disable thresholds as policy, and recommends sharing data contracts rather than one parser or UI shell.
gaps.residual_unknowns: This validation is doc-level only in iteration 2 because phase 5 external source inspection was outside the approved external-read scope.

gaps.title: Empirical validation of the 11.2x token claim

attempts.result: Phase 2 docs already said the 11.2x headline was README-only and that tokenStats was heuristic rather than benchmarked.
External source confirms it: README publishes 11.2x, tokens.ts computes exploration savings from fixed weights and chars/4 output estimates, and eval.ts only measures precision/recall/F1.

evidence.quote: Phase 2 already classified the 11.2x claim as README-only and unreproducible.
README publishes the three SaaS rows and the 11.2x average headline.
Token savings come from chars/4 output estimates plus fixed exploration weights.
The eval harness compares routes, models, env vars, components, and middleware only.

gaps.answer: There is no empirical Claude/GPT validation in this checkout. The 11.2x headline is a README benchmark built from heuristic token math, while the shipped eval harness validates detector accuracy only. The honest closure is negative: the claim is unvalidated, not merely under-documented.
gaps.residual_unknowns: No tokenizer-backed or model-backed benchmark exists for the public fixtures, so claim replacement would require new instrumentation, not more rereading.

gaps.title: tRPC / Fastify contract enrichment despite AST routing

attempts.result: Phase 2 docs said tRPC had AST route extraction but zero enrichment, while Fastify was routed through the generic TypeScript regex helper.
The contracts switch has no trpc case at all, and Fastify is grouped with Hono/Express/NestJS inside the same Hono-biased enrichTSRoute regex path.

evidence.quote: Phase 2 already described tRPC as unenriched and the shared TS helper as Hono-biased.
Fastify enters the generic TS branch; trpc is absent from the switch entirely.
The TS helper only looks for three regex patterns centered on Hono-style code.
AST routing explicitly supports trpc.

gaps.answer: Closed as a real implementation gap. tRPC gets AST route discovery but no contract pass at all. Fastify is not fully missing, but it only receives the same generic Hono-skewed regex enrichment as several other TS frameworks, so its contract surface is shallow and not framework-specific.
gaps.residual_unknowns: The exact miss rate is still unmeasured because there are no enrichment-focused fixtures or tests.

gaps.title: Go regex+brace-tracking labeled confidence: ast

attempts.result: Phase 2 already suspected a mislabel because Go was described as brace-tracking plus regex with no go/parser.
The source comment explicitly says structured parsing uses brace tracking and regex, yet the Go extractors stamp confidence:'ast' under a binary ast|regex enum.

evidence.quote: Phase 2 stated that Go was not truly AST-backed and that the label was misleading.
The file header says Go parsing is brace-tracking plus regex, not compiler AST.
Route extraction returns confidence:'ast' anyway.
The shared enum only permits ast or regex.

gaps.answer: This is a mislabel, not pending AST support. The implementation is a structured regex parser constrained by a binary confidence enum, and it currently advertises that result as ast. The honest fix would be a third label such as structured or parser-assisted.
gaps.residual_unknowns: None for the status question; only the remediation shape remains open.

gaps.title: Monorepo support beyond pnpm + npm workspaces

attempts.result: Phase 2 already narrowed support to pnpm-workspace.yaml and package.json workspaces, with explicit warnings about turbo/nx/lerna gaps.
Scanner code confirms only those two workspace sources are read, and workspace data is rolled up into one root ProjectInfo rather than preserved as a fuller graph.

evidence.quote: Phase 2 documented the limited monorepo inputs and warned about missing manager branches.
Monorepo detection checks only package workspaces or pnpm-workspace.yaml.
Workspace patterns come only from pnpm-workspace.yaml or package.json workspaces.

gaps.answer: Support is genuinely limited to npm-style workspaces and pnpm-workspace manifests. That creates a real omission risk for turbo, nx, lerna, or Rush repos that do not mirror workspace membership into those files. The silent-data-loss rate, however, is still unmeasured because no such fixture exists here.
gaps.residual_unknowns: No turbo/nx/lerna fixture or failure trace in the checkout quantifies how often routes or models would be silently missed.

gaps.title: Blast-radius BFS leak and heuristic schema-impact false-positive rate

attempts.result: Phase 2 already isolated both source-level problems: the BFS depth leak and the db-tag-based schema overlay heuristic.
External source confirms the false-positive mechanisms, but the shipped eval harness never scores blast-radius output, so no measured FP rate exists.

evidence.quote: Phase 2 documented both the off-by-one traversal bug and the heuristic schema overlay.
Dependents are added before the queued node's depth is checked.
Any affected file with a db-tagged route can mark every schema as affected.
The eval harness scores routes, models, env vars, components, and middleware only.

gaps.answer: The failure modes are source-confirmed, so the qualitative risk is real: depth can leak one hop too far and schema impact can over-fire whenever an affected file owns any db-tagged route. But the actual false-positive rate remains unknown because blast radius is not benchmarked anywhere in the shipped eval harness.
gaps.residual_unknowns: No blast-radius fixture, ground-truth comparison, or unit test quantifies frequency or severity of false positives.
